behaviors.description: The clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) present in the contract
behaviors.justification: The extractor's ability to find and classify key terms depends on the real LLM's natural language understanding and information extraction capability. Mocking the extractor would hide failures where the actual model misses key terms embedded in complex clause language, confuses similar clause types, or fails to locate non-standard formatting. The test must exercise the real LLM to validate it can consistently identify all term types across varied contract structures.
behaviors.test_description: Test the clause extractor on a real contract containing explicit instances of each key term type: identifiable parties, effective/signature dates, specific obligations with actions and deadlines, and defined penalties/remedies. Verify all identified terms match the expected locations and are correctly classified. Run multiple times to account for LLM non-determinism and verify findings are consistent across runs. Use contracts with varied formatting (standard vs. non-standard clause organization) to catch edge cases.

behaviors.description: The risk assessor flags language that would be problematic under the configured risk policy, even when the problematic phrasing is indirect or euphemistic
behaviors.justification: Detecting subtle language variations—especially euphemistic or indirect problematic phrasing—requires real LLM semantic reasoning. The behavior explicitly requires understanding non-obvious risk language. A mock could be programmed to pass any test we write, but the real LLM must genuinely understand semantic implications. Mocking at Unit or Integration level would hide failures where the LLM misses euphemisms or doesn't grasp the risk policy's intent.
behaviors.test_description: Test the risk assessor with a configured risk policy (e.g., 'flag unlimited liability clauses'). Provide clauses that violate the policy in both direct forms ('no liability limit') and indirect/euphemistic forms ('reasonable limitation of remedies', 'liability capped at contract value'). Verify the assessor flags both variants. Check for true positives (correctly flagged clauses) and false negatives (missed problematic language). Test with multiple risk policies to verify policy compliance understanding, and test edge cases where problematic language is deeply embedded in longer sentences.

behaviors.description: The compliance checker correctly determines whether the contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction
behaviors.justification: Regulatory compliance assessment depends critically on the LLM's knowledge of jurisdiction-specific legal requirements. We cannot reliably mock this without building a complete and accurate regulatory database. Different jurisdictions have vastly different requirements (e.g., California vs. Texas labor law). Testing requires the real LLM to demonstrate it actually understands the regulatory landscape for the target jurisdiction and can identify missing required disclosures, clauses, or certifications.
behaviors.test_description: Test the compliance checker with a contract for a specified jurisdiction (e.g., California data privacy law). Include test cases with contracts that: (1) contain all required regulatory elements (passes compliance), (2) omit specific required clauses or disclosures (fails), (3) have ambiguous or incomplete regulatory language (edge case). Verify the checker correctly identifies which requirements are met and which are missing. Test multiple jurisdictions with different legal frameworks to validate knowledge breadth. Cross-check identified requirements against published regulatory frameworks for accuracy.

behaviors.description: When two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments (e.g., extractor says 'standard' but risk assessor says 'high risk'), the merge step preserves both assessments with attribution
behaviors.justification: The merge logic itself is deterministic—given reviewer outputs with conflicts, it should produce the same merged report each time. However, the merge step must correctly coordinate outputs from multiple reviewers without data loss or attribution errors. Testing requires reviewer outputs (real or constructed) flowing through the merge step to verify: (1) both conflicting assessments are present in the output, (2) each assessment is correctly attributed to its source reviewer, (3) no data is lost or duplicated. This is an integration concern—multiple modules must hand off data correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Construct test contracts where the three reviewers will naturally or deliberately produce conflicting assessments of the same clause. For example: a clause the extractor categorizes as 'standard terms' but the risk assessor flags as 'high risk' under the policy. (Construct realistic reviewer output objects or use a test contract that reliably triggers this.) Verify the merged report contains both assessments with clear attribution (e.g., "extractor: standard", "risk_assessor: high risk"). Test edge cases: multiple conflicts on the same clause, conflicts on different clauses, all three reviewers disagreeing on a single clause, complete agreement. Verify the merge output is lossless and properly formatted.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — dispatching to all three reviewers, collecting results, merging into a unified report — completes correctly for a real contract without losing any individual reviewer's findings
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires multiple agentic steps (three independent LLM-based reviewers) coordinated together in sequence/parallel. Testing only individual reviewers (Agentic) would miss coordination failures: dispatch errors, timeout/partial result collection, merge failures that compound across multiple reviewers. The end-to-end risk is at the workflow level—can the system reliably orchestrate three LLM calls, collect their results, and merge them losslessly? This requires real LLM calls to all three reviewers to validate the full pipeline works together.
behaviors.test_description: Test the full pipeline with a real contract that is complex enough to produce distinct findings from each reviewer. Verify: (1) all three reviewers are successfully dispatched (concurrently or sequentially per design), (2) all three complete and results are collected without timeout or partial failure, (3) every finding from every reviewer appears in the final merged report, (4) no findings are dropped, duplicated, or corrupted during the merge. Measure latency and resource usage to ensure parallel dispatch does not introduce bottlenecks. Test with contracts of varying complexity and size. Verify the unified report is complete and correctly attributed.
